name: Konohanasakuya-hime
description: Konohanasakuya-hime, also known as Sakuya-bime, is a prominent figure in Japanese mythology, revered as the goddess of Mount Fuji and all blossoming flowers, particularly cherry blossoms (sakura). Her name translates to "blossom-princess-shining-radiantly." She embodies delicate beauty, transient life, and the ephemeral nature of existence, mirroring the short-lived but breathtaking bloom of the cherry blossom. She is often associated with volcanoes, specifically Mount Fuji, and her fiery temperament is linked to the mountain's volcanic activity. Her stories emphasize themes of mortality, doubt, and the power of divine lineage. She is worshipped for safe childbirth, happy marriage, and protection against fire. Her shrines are often located near volcanoes and blooming cherry trees, reinforcing her multifaceted symbolism. Her appeal lies in her potent combination of gentleness and formidable power.
subtitle: Blossom-Princess-Shining-Radiantly

keyMyths.title: The Doubt of Ninigi
keyMyths.description: Konohanasakuya-hime married Ninigi-no-Mikoto, grandson of the sun goddess Amaterasu. After she became pregnant, Ninigi doubted the child's paternity, suspecting it was not his due to the brevity of their courtship. To prove her fidelity and divine lineage, Konohanasakuya-hime entered a tightly sealed hut and set it on fire, proclaiming that if the children were truly Ninigi's, they would be unharmed. The three children emerged unscathed, thus proving her innocence and their divine heritage. This myth highlights themes of doubt, faith, and the confirmation of divine lineage through trial by fire.
keyMyths.source: Aston, W. G. (1896). Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697.

keyMyths.title: The Choice of Immortality
keyMyths.description: Amaterasu offered Ninigi two sisters: Iwanaga-hime (Rock-Princess), representing eternal life, and Konohanasakuya-hime (Blossom-Princess), representing earthly beauty and prosperity. Ninigi chose Konohanasakuya-hime, rejecting immortality for the fleeting beauty of the blossom. This decision is said to be the reason why human life is short and mortal, like the cherry blossom. Had Ninigi chosen Iwanaga-hime, humans would have been granted eternal life.
keyMyths.source: Chamberlain, B. H. (1919). Kojiki: Records of Ancient Matters.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Mount Fuji
keyMyths.description: While no direct account describes Konohanasakuya-hime creating Mount Fuji, she is intrinsically linked to the mountain. Some interpretations suggest her fiery nature is responsible for the volcanic activity that formed the mountain over millennia. She is considered its protector and embodiment, and her presence is felt in the mountain's majestic beauty and powerful energy. Shrines dedicated to her are located on and around the mountain, solidifying her connection to it.
keyMyths.source: Picken, S. D. B. (1994). Essentials of Shinto: An Analytical Guide to Principal Teachings.

associations.name: Fire
associations.significance: Represents her fiery birth and the power she wields, as well as the volcanic nature of Mount Fuji.

associations.name: Pink
associations.significance: Associated with cherry blossoms, symbolizing beauty, love, and the fleeting nature of life.

associations.name: Beauty
associations.significance: Embodies the ideal of feminine beauty and grace in Japanese culture.

associations.name: Transience
associations.significance: Reflects the ephemeral nature of cherry blossoms and the impermanence of life.

associations.name: Moon
associations.significance: Linked to lunar cycles and feminine energy, complementing her role as a goddess of fertility and beauty.
Considered a lucky number in Japan and often associated with deities and good fortune, aligning with her revered status.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Devotees offer sake, rice, and flowers, particularly cherry blossoms, at shrines dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime. These offerings are meant to honor her beauty and seek her blessings for fertility, safe childbirth, and protection from volcanic eruptions.

worshipPractices.name: Shrine Visits
worshipPractices.description: Pilgrimages to Mount Fuji and Sengen shrines (dedicated to her) are common. Worshippers pray for good fortune and pay respect to the mountain's sacred presence, believing Konohanasakuya-hime protects the region.

festivals.name: Cherry Blossom Festivals (Hanami)
festivals.description: While not exclusively for Konohanasakuya-hime, these festivals celebrate the beauty of cherry blossoms, a central symbol associated with her. People gather under the blooming trees to appreciate their beauty and reflect on the transient nature of life.

festivals.name: Sengen Shrine Festivals
festivals.description: Many Sengen shrines, dedicated to Konohanasakuya-hime, hold annual festivals featuring traditional music, dance, and processions. These festivals honor the goddess and seek her continued protection and blessings for the community.

cultural.modernLegacy: Konohanasakuya-hime remains a popular figure in Japanese culture, symbolizing beauty, grace, and the ephemeral nature of life. She is featured in art, literature, and popular media, continuing to inspire awe and reverence. She represents the beauty of Japan and the spirit of the nation.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Konohanasakuya-hime's origins are rooted in ancient Japanese mythology. She is the daughter of Ōyamatsumi, the god of mountains. Her story is prominently featured in the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, two of Japan's oldest historical texts. According to the myths, Ninigi-no-Mikoto, grandson of the sun goddess Amaterasu, encountered Konohanasakuya-hime and was captivated by her beauty. He asked for her hand in marriage, and Ōyamatsumi offered both Konohanasakuya-hime and her elder sister, Iwa-Naga-hime (Rock-Long-Princess). Ninigi chose only Konohanasakuya-hime, leading Ōyamatsumi to lament that Ninigi's descendants would have lives as fleeting as cherry blossoms instead of the eternal life represented by Iwa-Naga-hime. This choice is said to be the reason for the mortality of humans.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: One of the most significant myths surrounding Konohanasakuya-hime involves her husband, Ninigi-no-Mikoto, doubting the paternity of their child. To prove her fidelity and the divine lineage of her offspring, she entered a tightly sealed hut and set it ablaze, declaring that if the children were truly Ninigi's, they would be unharmed. Miraculously, she emerged unscathed with her three sons: Hoderi (sea luck), Hosuseri (fire luck), and Hoori (fire subdue). This trial by fire solidified her divine status and proved the legitimacy of her children. This myth highlights her strength, resilience, and unwavering commitment to her family. It also reinforces her association with fire, symbolizing her power and purity. This story is a cornerstone of Japanese mythology, demonstrating the power and importance of the gods.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Konohanasakuya-hime is primarily worshipped at Sengen shrines throughout Japan, with the most prominent being the Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha at the base of Mount Fuji. These shrines are dedicated to her as the protector of the mountain and the goddess of blossoming. Worship practices typically involve offering prayers for safe childbirth, marital harmony, and protection from volcanic eruptions. Devotees often present offerings of sake, rice, and flowers, particularly cherry blossoms, to honor her beauty and seek her blessings. The veneration of Konohanasakuya-hime is deeply intertwined with the reverence for Mount Fuji itself, considered a sacred and powerful symbol of Japan. Her cult reflects the importance of nature worship and the belief in the divine presence within the natural world.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Konohanasakuya-hime's legacy extends far beyond her role as a mythological figure. She embodies the Japanese appreciation for beauty, nature, and the ephemeral aspects of life. Her association with cherry blossoms has cemented her place in Japanese art, literature, and popular culture. She represents the ideal of feminine beauty and strength, inspiring artists and writers for centuries. Modern depictions of Konohanasakuya-hime often portray her as a graceful and powerful goddess, reflecting her enduring appeal and relevance in contemporary society. Her story continues to resonate with people who seek to connect with nature, appreciate beauty, and find meaning in the transient moments of life. She is a symbol of Japan itself.

shortDescription: Goddess of Mount Fuji and cherry blossoms in Japanese mythology, embodying beauty and transient life. She is revered for her grace and power.
